By the time the integration test starts, we will have energized the power supply.
This time next week, we will be running a dry run of the failover procedure.
The supplier will have submitted the look-ahead schedule before the next milestone review.
By September, the team will have been working nights for six weeks.
We will have closed them by Friday.
We will be running the dry run next week.
What are you going to do about the interfaces?
We should have flagged that interface earlier.

Right, João, let's get straight into it, I've got another call at half past. Where are we on site readiness? Because by the twelfth I need that power supply energized, or the integration test doesn't happen.
I want to make sure I'm following — the deadline you're referring to is the twelfth, and that is for site readiness, not for the integration test itself. Is that right?

Correct. Readiness on the twelfth, test from the fifteenth, and we'll be running a dry run of the failover the week after that. The ramp-up to full service starts in November, and that date does not move.

Understood. Then by the fifteenth we will have energized the power supply and closed the two open actions on the cable route, and I will have confirmed site readiness in writing. We will be working nights from the eighth to hold that date.
Good. And the interfaces? I don't want to discover during the test that nobody was managing them.
Interface management sits with me. Before I commit to anything else — could you run the acceptance dates by me one more time? I want to factor them in before I send you the look-ahead schedule.

Provisional acceptance, end of Q3. Final acceptance twelve months later, and the defects liability period runs in between. And João — the manual workarounds get phased out before provisional acceptance, not after. I've seen too many projects where they quietly become the design.

Just to confirm — are you saying that no workaround survives provisional acceptance? Then by the thirtieth we will have cleared every outstanding item on that list, and you will have the three-week look-ahead tonight.
By the twelfth we will have confirmed site readiness in writing.
This time next week we will be running a dry run of the failover procedure.
Could you run that by me one more time?
Just to confirm — are you saying that the workarounds come out before provisional acceptance?
I want to make sure I'm following: the deadline you're referring to is the twelfth, not the fifteenth.

João, it is Fiona, I have got two minutes between meetings so I will be quick. Two things, and one of them you will not like. The board asked me this morning, after our call, to bring provisional acceptance forward. So it is no longer the thirtieth of September. It is the twenty-third, and I am telling you now rather than letting you read it in a document next week. Nothing else moves. Site readiness on the twelfth, the integration test from the fifteenth, the dry run of the failover the week after, and the ramp-up in November, which was never negotiable anyway. Second thing, and this one is mine, not yours. The interface document from the rolling stock supplier is three weeks late and it is my side that is late, not yours. I am not going to pretend otherwise and I am not going to ask you to absorb it quietly. What I am asking is that you hold the test date, and that you tell me the moment it starts to hurt, not three weeks after it has started hurting. Send me the three-week look-ahead tonight. And João, please, do not build me a schedule that carries a workaround into provisional acceptance, and do not give me a date that has not factored in the night-shift restrictions on that site. I would rather have a date I can trust than a date I like.

Three rules, before your next international call, because the engineers who lose these calls are almost never the ones with the weakest English. First rule. The fast speaker is not testing you. She is not thinking about your English at all. She is late, she has three projects and a board meeting, and she talks that way to the man sitting next to her, who is English. Speed is not an exam. It is her Tuesday. Once you stop hearing it as a judgment, you stop freezing, and the moment you stop freezing you start catching about a third more than you did. Second rule. Stop hunting for the auxiliary. You were taught will have and will be, and in real speech they are simply not there. Will have collapses into something like willuv, we will have becomes we'll've, and will be almost disappears into the verb behind it. But the tense does not disappear with them, because it survives at the end of the phrase, in the participle. Closed. Energized. Running. Phased out. Listen to the last word, not the first, and the grammar comes back. Third rule, and this one is worth money. Never agree to a date you have not repeated back out loud. Not a nod, not an of course, not a yes yes. Say it back. Just to confirm, the twelfth, not the fifteenth. Could you run that by me one more time. It takes five seconds, it makes you sound like a professional rather than a nervous one, and I have watched a project lose a crew, a night shift and a whole test window because a very good engineer was too polite to spend those five seconds.
